title: Same Word, Different Rules
deck: Two people can use the same word while applying different tests. This map shows where the argument stops sharing a rule.
final_explanation: The speakers use different criteria for the same category. More examples alone will not resolve the dispute until the decision rule is made explicit.
primary_hinge: Is racial classification sufficient, or does context determine the category?
primary_question: What makes a race-specific institution or action racist?
criteria_under_dispute: whether the same comparison rule is applied across cases
source_disclosure: Based on an anonymized public exchange. Claims have been paraphrased to isolate the reasoning structure.
claim_boundary: DSM deterministically renders an anonymized semantic-collision fixture across baseline, pressure, and drift states.
prohibited_claim: DSM does not determine whether a person, institution, or statement is racist.

exchange.speaker: Speaker A
exchange.text: One proposed definition treats racism as prejudice combined with institutional power.

exchange.speaker: Speaker A
exchange.text: The speaker then points to accepted race-specific institutions and asks whether the rule is being applied consistently.

exchange.speaker: Speaker B
exchange.text: The reply rejects the comparison as a false equivalence because context may change the category.

exchange.speaker: Drift marker
exchange.text: The exchange then shifts from evaluating the comparison to evaluating the intellectual honesty of the person making it.

nodes.label: Racism requires institutional power.
nodes.meaning: One proposed category rule defines racism as prejudice combined with institutional power.

nodes.label: Some race-specific institutions are accepted without being classified as racist.
nodes.meaning: The speaker presents several socially accepted race-specific institutions and initiatives.

nodes.label: The rule is being applied inconsistently.
nodes.meaning: The speaker argues that the accepted examples conflict with the stated definition or its usual application.

nodes.label: The examples should be judged under the same classification rule.
nodes.meaning: The inconsistency claim only works if the compared cases belong under one shared test.

nodes.label: The examples are a false equivalence.
nodes.meaning: The responder rejects the comparison rather than accepting the proposed inconsistency.

nodes.label: Purpose, power, exclusion, coercion, effect, and history may change the classification.
nodes.meaning: The false-equivalence response implies that racial classification alone may be insufficient.

nodes.label: What makes two race-specific cases meaningfully comparable?
nodes.meaning: Neither speaker provides a complete comparison rule.

nodes.label: Is racial classification sufficient, or does context determine the category?
nodes.meaning: This is the unresolved decision boundary beneath the visible disagreement.

nodes.label: Disagreement becomes a judgment about intellectual honesty.
nodes.meaning: The conversation moves from evaluating the comparison to evaluating the person making it.

nodes.label: Unresolved semantic collision
nodes.meaning: The argument cannot resolve until a shared category and comparison rule is selected.

edges.qualification: Only if the examples fall under the same comparison rule.

states.label: Baseline
states.caption: One speaker proposes an inconsistency, but the comparison rule remains implicit.

states.label: Pressure
states.caption: The reply rejects the comparison, revealing that the speakers are using different tests.

states.label: Drift
states.caption: The exchange moves from examining the rule to judging the speaker. The underlying definition remains unresolved.
